text: If the text is small on some of these slides and you want to follow along, or you want some
spoilers, you can get the slides locally. I also just posted the link on my account.
Yeah, thanks everybody for coming. I'm going to jump right in. I'm Fig or Phil. You might know
me as a bad example. And last year when I was here, I had shipped Constellation. And since then,
I've done a few other things. This talk is about community infrastructure and specifically small
scale community infrastructure. And I was trying to think about how to define that scope. And I
couldn't, like there's some obvious things that I wanted to say. And like for the purpose of this
talk, I ended up just settling on things that's kind of weird that you can just do.
App Proto has a lot of this, but I'm just going to be talking about it in the context of small
scale infra. You can relay the whole firehose yourself just as like a random person. And that's
like kind of weird. I think Twitter had open access to a firehose early on. And that was like really
wild. App Proto lets you run the firehose yourself in a much, much more powerful way.
And on a full network scale, like Blue Sky, a major social media company could use my relay for the
day if they wanted. And there's a lot of reason they wouldn't. But those reasons are not that it
wouldn't work. It would actually like everything, you wouldn't notice a difference.
You can also just index everything. So that's Constellation. Every single social interaction
is a backlink in App Proto. And all of those go into my house, into my living room. And this
googly-eyed Raspberry Pi takes note of them.
I'm going to talk a little bit about Constellation. It's really simple. So I made this diagram that
makes it look really complicated. The firehose comes in with all the posts and blocks and likes
and everything that's happening on the network called records. Constellation opens each of
those up and searches through for anything that looks like a link. And it doesn't matter what
type it is. If something looks like a link, it will take note of where it came from. And then just sort
of put those in buckets of backlinks. So you can say, what are all the replies to this post? And
Constellation can tell you because it's indexed a bucket of replies to every single post that's
ever happened that you can just ask about.
So Rudy mentioned the follower counts that are really important to everybody. And this is an
example of where you need that kind of aggregation across the network to be able to just show this
number on someone's profile. So that's something that you can get from Blue Sky. Their sort of like
server backend has an API that can get you lots of information about the profile, including doing
this kind of network aggregation of everybody that follows Sharpie. You just give it their identity and
you say get profile and you get an answer back. So the same thing with Constellation. You have to give
it sort of one more thing, which is like, where are the links coming from? So in this case, we're asking
about follows. And within a follow record, the link to define a follow is the subject of the follow.
And then Constellation will kind of give you exactly the same kind of information, but in a
generic general purpose way that's not specific to Blue Sky, works for every app, Proto app.
People use it. And people use it a lot more recently as well, which I'll come back to in a second.
This is all pretty low cost to run because it's running on Raspberry Pi at my house. So something like
spores.garden, when you have it show you the series of steps that one of your mushroom spores has,
or whatever it is, has like gotten to you, it will reach out to my living room, Cassiopeia up there,
and it will tell them. There's a problem with this, which is my internet is from an internet
service provider that gives me a dynamic IP address. And there's also just one Raspberry Pi. And if it
crashes then a lot of apps have issues.
So, I had a support to buy this little micro PC that runs a hot failover, also at my house.
And if the Raspberry Pi has any issues, then we just failover to the secondary instance. So,
we have like a little bit of redundancy failover happening here. I also don't actually really want
to put my even dynamic IP directly on the internet. So, I have this little $5 a month cloud server VPS
that acts as a reverse proxy, gives me some nice things, like I do TLS termination there,
caching and rate limiting, and then it's just the upstream configuration in NGINX over tail scale to
get to my house and I don't have to worry about dynamic IPs. So, we're at $5 a month to do this.
But we also have a new single point of failure, which is that $5 a month VPS that needs to be
updated and restarted and stuff from time to time. So, we do the same thing.
I put one on DigitalOcean and one on Vulture, so Constellation can survive an entire platform-wide
outage on DigitalOcean if it goes down, as long as Vulture and DigitalOcean don't go down at the same
time. How do you load balance in front of that? It's not a load balancer for reasons that if anybody
wants to talk about, we can talk about after. But for the moment, it's just DNS-based load balancing,
which in practice with two instances works really well. DNS is pretty reliable, but I guess I would
get taken down by a DNS outage. And things just kind of like automatically fail over between them.
So, this is up to $10 a month now for Constellation. Running at home still has challenges. Last summer,
the fiber connection to my house was cut at the distribution box accidentally, presumably.
And so, I was able at the time to like tether over cellular for a couple days until a tech could come
out to fix it. I don't think at the current scale of use that would actually work. But I'm looking at
maybe getting a cable secondary internet connection. I had a power outage in January that was longer than
my battery backup UPS runtime. So, we had a one-hour downtime of Constellation for that.
But it's very cheap to do all this, and it's very low-tech. And the thing that I like about low-tech is
that nothing really goes wrong generally. And when they do, they're usually really easy to
figure out what's going on and debug. And I guess just in case anybody's still worried about this,
like running at my house, there's also public backups of the Constellation database that are
pushed every four hours to object storage. You can get it yourself and restore a full copy of
Constellation to run if you want. And it also means that if something catastrophic happens at my
house, I can be back up in the cloud within some hours. But I don't have to pay for that in the
meantime other than the object storage. We're going to get out of Microcosm specific in a second, but
I did want to just kind of follow Constellation a little further than I did last year, which is that
it's only storing the links. It's not storing any like record contents from Constellation. So,
when you ask for all the replies to a post, you might get like this thread of many responses,
but you have to go and fetch each of those individually to render a UI like this.
And in the protocol, we have get record sync or repo versions. And if the PDS has that record is
offline, then you might be in luck if you put your request through Slingshot, which is an edge record
cache. It keeps a cache of the firehose and then a cache of requested identities and records from
them so that you have much faster responses for get record. And if it's in the cache, then you don't
need to worry about availability. And you can also proxy your request Constellation through Slingshot and
have it hydrate everything on this way out. So, you can do one single request to get that thread UI
fully hydrated on the way out. Rudy mentioned like record hydration is just that one above the water
thing of building it up to you. And I don't want to pretend that I'm saying anything otherwise, but
I think this can get you pretty far. I'm going to just show these two graphs from data that I pulled
yesterday from another microcosm service called UFOs, which tracks some statistics about every lexicon
that comes through the firehose. I think this one might surprise some people given how much hype there
is around the atmosphere. The top of that y-axis is 15,000 unique people. We've never broken 15,000
unique people using non-blue sky apps within a week. It's small. It's early. And like the trend from
like after the conference last year for most of the year was not exciting. I don't actually, I didn't have
time to figure out what this one spike was earlier, but I do want to just like point to that last kind of
two or three months at the end there that kind of corresponded to the rise that I have in stuff using
microcosm. The orange one with question marks is there because that's actually only one day of the weekly
bucket. So this bucket is still rising up. And I, and I think, I think this kind of more recent thing.
Yeah. Oh, nice. Nice. Awesome. Awesome. Yeah.
These charts kind of show something interesting together, like these two, which are basically the
same thing versus the one on the last slide, which is that even though uptake among users has been slow,
uptake among developers has been steady. And I think that's why this like lag and now we're seeing
the growth. Like I, I think it's early. I don't think these are actually pessimistic slides.
But yeah, so more than 10 weekly active users using a given lexicon or data type bucketed, we've got
like, you know, almost 80 of them now, lexicon groups that have more than 10 people using them at least one
time in a week. Okay. I'm doing okay on time. I think so I'm going to say this part.
I said I would talk about economics. It's kind of boring, but end of 2025, I pulled some numbers and I
was getting around $200 after fees from people sponsoring me on GitHub and Ko-Fi or coffee or however you say it,
which with some of the more expensive cloud servers for Slingshot and stuff netted me about $33 a month.
I'm getting rich. I've gotten some grants that have helped. Shout out Haifa, who gave me a really generous
donation recently. And UpCloud sponsored a lot of the PLC stuff I was doing for hosting.
I'm going to be building Hubble soon, which has come with a grant from Blue Sky and will come
with operating expenses for the year I have to run it. I don't know how much it's going to cost yet,
because I haven't built it. But in 2024, running the close comparable existing thing wasn't cheap.
Since I mentioned relays, so we had that $153 a month in 2024. Last year at the conference,
there was somebody trying to run $2 million to run a relay. We can skip past that.
Future ran it on a Raspberry Pi after that because non-archival relays, so not what I'm doing with
Hubble, are cheap. I got one up in the cloud for $30 a month the month after that. Got it down to $18
on a different provider. Brian did it for $34 a month and wrote it up in a nice post. And last month,
I got one working on a under $5 a month VPS. The network is growing, but it's weird that you can just
run infra pretty affordably. These are some other ways I think I might be able to make money and some
ideas. But this is actually what I want to get to, which is, I don't know, why do people do weird
things in AppProto? I didn't think my answers alone would be very interesting. I reached out to 10
people that are running relays or APIs or other things on a very small scale. And I got 10 responses
back. Shout out to all those people, some in this room. Thank you. And yeah, I realized that
I had never asked any of them why they do it, and I didn't really know.
So I tried to consolidate some of those answers here. A lot of people do it for fun,
to learn things, or they needed something for themselves and decided to open it up,
or to show what's possible. These were like recurring themes.
I think some of the real idealism came out in these. I think this is the best chance we've had
in a long time to make this social internet more independent for the few mostly evil big tech companies.
I asked people how they pay for it. Almost 100% out of pocket at this kind of like small scale,
which I guess makes sense. But people do that by running things cheap. Some people,
one person did it out of pocket because they can't accept money or grants due to work or other reasons.
GitHub and CoFi support to do this kind of stuff is a huge recurring theme across everybody that answered
this. Small grants and in some cases people are able to run things that fit within free offerings from services.
So someone said they're blessed to have enough GitHub and CoFi donations to cover costs.
Yeah, small grants and then out of pocket and then supported.
Again, if you want to read more of these, you can get the slides.
I asked what people thought about sort of like small indie infrastructure in the long term,
keeping it going or like what they think about when they think about winding it down when maybe people
start using it. So it either has to become too expensive or I get bored of posting my infra.
Neither are true at the moment. Or I think if things go well, people like you and Bailey and others
will be able to make a living providing hosting. We can dream.
Yeah, unless things get massively expensive, then people kind of just want to keep it online.
Not everybody had optimistic takes here.
Keep stuff running because I have to and it has become somewhat load-bearing for the ecosystem,
was a response.
But people also expressed a lot of thought in how they were thinking about how people might get off of
their services if they had to wind it down. So providing like open source codes that people can
run it themselves and it's cheap because it has to be cheap because these are small-scale operators
and trying to make those migration paths exist.
Yeah, I really don't see how developers ever break even or make money off projects like this
and the company is too inconsistent with who it chooses to fund or how often it funds them.
It was a thought.
I asked what people thought the future of social infrastructure will look like.
Medium and large-scale community PDSs like Black Sky, North Sky, Euro Sky.
I would include Bridgie in here, but that came up a lot. I think people really do see that as the
way it's going to go. Personally, I think we're also going to see like a lot more
of people like these dozen or so like independent tiny small-scale operators as well.
Maybe it'll be more centralized, more mindless. Maybe I'll be wrong. Yeah.
How could communities or companies better support came up a little bit.
Some of the coolest things to run that maybe is only at a prototype stage needs a lot of resources
and more than you might be able to do at like a very small-scale IndieInfra operator.
And so that came up a few times. Like, is there a way we can kind of pool community resources to
provide that sort of like available infrastructure to try some experiments that would be driven on
that sort of like smaller individual basis?
When I was getting through these, I also realized that like I have a bunch of people supporting me
on GitHub and KoFi and I've never really like directly asked any of them like, "Why do you support me?"
So I got eight people to reply to some questions that I sent to people who are supporting
folks running small-scale IndieInfra.
These are in the reverse order. So what those people, so supporters of small-scale IndieInfra think
that the future of social infrastructure will look like more big companies involved in AppProto.
This is all like AppProto. A mix of software as a service and public services.
I'm pretty hopeful about that one and optimistic. We have some examples that people pointed to like
Debian, Wikimedia, a couple others that I forgot to put on here.
And yeah, people don't think there will be a future where social Infra isn't free for the
public to use. I think that's not disproven so far by AppProto. And yeah, shout out to QuickSlice,
where you can run your own backend, self-host, pull in just what you need from the big network,
if that's what you're trying to do.
Laptops with batteries, with swelling batteries, yet handle 1000+ concurrent users just fine.
I think this came from like the theme of we can just make things more cheap by putting effort
into making them efficient, as we've seen by small-scale operators in AppProto.
So from the funders' perspective, supporting small-scale people, thinking about the future
and winding it down, I really like this one. Not on my watch. As it expands, financial sustainability
might become more challenging, but due to the immense benefits it provides, I'm optimistic that
solutions can be found. I kind of feel this way too. I think as the whole network grows,
the resources of everybody to support small-scale things also grows.
Probably there will be some business models or some donation-based nonprofits that can support
what's needed in the long term. But yeah. And some people feel comfortable donating right now,
but don't know how long that will be the case for themselves or others. So again, mixed,
it's not all like pure optimism around this, but I think everybody is like kind of, I would say,
guarded optimism or cautious for the most part. Open-sourced. And I think like we talk about
the permissionlessness of AppProto a lot as well. And yeah, what ends up critical will be replaced if
needed. Um, because this is a really small-scale operations and you can just replace it. I'm trying
really hard not to say you can just do things. Why do people support public infra? The biggest one was
that they were in a position to be able to do it. Um, they want to see the atmosphere succeed,
and they think that very small-scale operators are a part of that. Um, the option to self-host came up a
lot. Um, just the fact that you can do that for these different components like PDS, of course, but
not just the PDS. Um, it provides that kind of assurance that people want. Um, this one was cool.
Like, I might not be using most of the services I sponsor for, but I know others are and may not
be able to sponsor it themselves. Like, I would say even more than the people running, uh, infrastructure,
the responses from people funding it were like really this kind of, uh, beautiful, um, community-oriented
sentiments around just, like, supporting the ecosystem. Um, this came up a lot. It's important
to support maintainers of projects I build on, especially when those projects aren't backed by
huge companies. Uh, yeah, I would say more than that, more of that. Um, and I'm going to close it on
this quote, which I liked, uh, more community-led than big business-led. I think this is like a high
bar to aim for, but, um, I, I think we can try.
Do you want to use "we can just try" as an alternate?
Uh, we have a couple minutes before lunch. Does anyone have a question for fake?
Um, with Constellation, what was the weirdest thing somebody did about it?
Oh, gosh.
I think I know the answer, but I'm not sure.
So, the question was, what's the weirdest thing somebody has done with Constellation?
Um, I should have prepared something for this, because people have done some really weird things
with Constellation. Um, I really love the kind of experiments people do, like Spore's Garden was
neat. Um, I think one of the just surprising ones that other people think of was Bailey's, uh, hooked up
his, uh, badge with the e-ink screen on it to both show the QR code for meet me, you and me at,
um, but then Bailey presses another button, and it shows all the people that Bailey's connected with,
and it queries that through Constellation, uh, and like, yeah, wild. So, but I want to hear what
you thought was the weirdest.
I'm remembering when Aviva and I were doing Deck Vulture and figuring out, okay,
no, we can do Scryful URIs. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. People, people often say, hey, you should
also index URLs, not just at URIs. And Constellation has, from day one, it indexes anything that looks
like a, a did, or an at URI, or any URI that parses like a URI. So, um, if you want to see all the
posts to your leaflet that are shared on Blue Sky as, like, link embeds, you can do that. And it also
means you can hack the system by, uh, putting, like, your own URI scheme, um, that you invent,
and just, like, create links that aren't real links, but Constellation aggregates them for you,
so then you can query it. You can do, kind of, like, arbitrary, uh, group, grouping and aggregation
with Constellation that way. Yeah, that's a good one.
Any more questions? I'm just going to add one more thing on the end of that, which is,
sometimes, um, people use Constellation in ways that aren't scalable, uh, like, trying to use it as a
leaderboard, um, by having every score link to a URL, and then doing a lot of client-side filtering,
and I think that's awesome. It won't scale forever, but if you're ever wondering, like,
is it okay if I can use Constellation for this, um, it's indexing everything that happens on Blue Sky,
so it can handle it. Like, whatever you're thinking about throwing at it, uh, yeah, just go for it.
Amazing. Thank you so much.
